stem: The curve \( [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} = [[k]] \) passes through the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \). Using implicit differentiation, what is \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} \) at that point?
solution: Differentiating \( [[a]]x^{2}+[[b]]y^{2}=[[k]] \) with respect to \( x \) gives \( 2([[a]])x + 2([[b]])y\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0 \). Solving for \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} \) and substituting \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the negative sign that comes from moving the \( x \)-term to the other side.
This inverts the correct ratio, computing \( -y/x \)-style terms swapped.
This keeps an extra, unneeded factor of 2 from the differentiated equation instead of letting it cancel.
This forgets to include \( y \) in the denominator entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The curve \( [[a]]xy + [[b]]x + [[c]]y = [[k]] \) passes through the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \). Using implicit differentiation, what is \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} \) at that point?
solution: Differentiating \( [[a]]xy+[[b]]x+[[c]]y=[[k]] \) with respect to \( x \) (product rule on \( xy \)) gives \( [[a]]y + [[a]]x\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} + [[b]] + [[c]]\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0 \). Solving for \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} \) and substituting \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the negative sign that comes from isolating \( dy/dx \) on one side.
This inverts the correct ratio of numerator and denominator.
This drops the constants \( b \) and \( c \) entirely, keeping only the \( xy \)-term's contribution.
This uses only the linear coefficients \( b,c \) and ignores the product-rule term from \( xy \) altogether.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The curve \( x^{[[n]]} + y^{[[n]]} = [[k]] \) passes through the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \). Using implicit differentiation, what is \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} \) at that point?
solution: Differentiating \( x^{[[n]]}+y^{[[n]]}=[[k]] \) with respect to \( x \) gives \( [[n]]x^{[[n]]-1} + [[n]]y^{[[n]]-1}\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0 \). Solving for \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} \) and substituting \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the negative sign that comes from isolating \( dy/dx \).
This inverts the correct ratio \( -(x/y)^{n-1} \).
This forgets to lower the exponent on \( y \) by one, leaving it at \( n \).
This omits the \( y^{n-1} \) term in the denominator entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The curve \( x e^{y} + y = [[k]] \) passes through the point \( ([[x0]], 0) \) (note \( e^{0}=1 \)). Using implicit differentiation, what is \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} \) at that point?
solution: Differentiating \( xe^{y}+y=[[k]] \) with respect to \( x \) (product rule on \( xe^{y}\)) gives \( e^{y} + xe^{y}\dfrac{dy}{dx} + \dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0 \). At \( ([[x0]],0) \), \( e^{0}=1 \), so \( 1 + [[x0]]\dfrac{dy}{dx} + \dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0 \), giving \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the negative sign and forgets the extra \( +1 \) from the plain \( y \) term in the denominator.
This has the right sign structure but forgets the extra \( +1 \) from the plain \( y \) term entirely.
This adds 2 instead of 1 to the denominator, over-correcting for the extra term.
This reports \( x_{0} \) itself instead of solving the implicit-differentiation equation.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The curve \( \dfrac{[[a]]}{x} + \dfrac{[[b]]}{y} = [[k]] \) passes through the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \). Using implicit differentiation, what is \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} \) at that point?
solution: Differentiating \( \dfrac{[[a]]}{x}+\dfrac{[[b]]}{y}=[[k]] \) with respect to \( x \) gives \( -\dfrac{[[a]]}{x^{2}} - \dfrac{[[b]]}{y^{2}}\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0 \). Solving for \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} \) and substituting \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the correct ratio, computing \( ay^2/(bx^2) \) with the wrong sign as well.
This inverts the correct ratio \( -ay^{2}/(bx^{2}) \).
This forgets to include the squared \( x \) and \( y \) factors entirely.
This uses \( x \) and \( y \) to the first power instead of squared.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
